mnemonics.title: Shading Methods Order
mnemonics.content: FGP - Flat, Gouraud, Phong (increasing complexity and realism)

mnemonics.title: Phong Model Components
mnemonics.content: ADS - Ambient, Diffuse, Specular (remember as 'Always Dark Streets')

mnemonics.title: Light Vectors
mnemonics.content: NLV - Normal (N), Light (L), View (V) - 'Never Leave Victoria' helps remember N, L, V vectors needed for lighting

acronyms.usage: Perpendicular direction to surface, determines light interaction
Direction from surface to light source
Direction of perfect specular reflection: R = 2(N·L)N - L
Used in Blinn-Phong: H = (L + V) / |L + V|

acronyms.expansion: Dot product of Normal and Light
acronyms.usage: Cosine of angle between normal and light; key to diffuse calculation

visualTips.visualization: Imagine holding a mirror flat under a light - maximum brightness. Tilt it away, brightness decreases proportionally to cosine of tilt angle.
Think of a shiny apple under a lamp. The bright spot (highlight) appears where the light reflects directly toward your eye. Move your head, highlight moves.
Gouraud: Paint vertex corners then blend colors across. Phong: Draw grid of normal arrows, blend them, then paint each cell with calculated color.
When surface faces light (angle < 90°), dot product is positive = light. When facing away (angle > 90°), dot product is negative = shadow.

analogies.analogy: Like painting an entire room wall with one bucket of paint - same color everywhere despite surface variations.
Like drawing a gradient on paper by coloring corners and blending inward with a brush - colors blend smoothly.
Like having a tiny paint roller for every square inch - each point gets its own custom color based on its exact angle to light.
Like the general brightness in a room with windows - not from any one source but from light bouncing around everywhere.
Like the glint on a wet road or car bumper - bright sharp spot that moves as you walk past.
